lines: Find a comfortable position. Sitting is ideal for this one, but whatever works. Take a moment to adjust. Settle in.
Breathe in through your nose for four.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for four.
One more. In... and a long, slow release.
Let the breathing go natural now.
We're going to begin with your body. Because today's meditation is about something your body already knows — the difference between gripping and releasing.
Start with your jaw. Is it clenched? Most people carry tension there without realizing it. Let it drop. Let your lips part just slightly.
Your shoulders. Are they lifted? Braced? Like you're expecting something to land on you? Let them fall. All the way down.
Your hands. Where are they? And what shape are they in? Are they fisted? Curled? Gripping the armrest, your own clothing, your own skin?

Now... make a fist. Both hands. Deliberately. Squeeze. Feel what that does to your whole body. Notice how the tension radiates — up your forearms, into your shoulders, into your jaw, your chest. Everything tightens when you grip.
This is how many of us move through life. Gripping. Holding on. Trying to control the outcome, manage the situation, prevent the thing we're afraid of. It's exhausting. And it doesn't work.
Keep your fists closed for another moment. And ask yourself: what am I gripping right now? Not with my hands — with my life. What am I trying to control?
Is it a relationship? An outcome at work? Your health? Your children's choices? Someone's opinion of you? The future? A timeline you set for yourself?
Name another one if it comes.

Notice what each of those things does to your body when you think about them. The tightening. The bracing. The clenching. Control lives in the body as tension. It masquerades as responsibility, but it feels like exhaustion.

Now... very slowly... begin to open your hands. Not all at once. Finger by finger. Feel the tendons release. Feel the palms soften. Feel the shift that happens in your shoulders, your chest, your belly as the grip lets go.
Open your hands all the way. Turn your palms upward if you can. Let them rest on your knees or at your sides, face up. Open. Empty. Receiving.

Feel the difference. The open hand and the clenched fist are not just different postures — they are different ways of being in the world. One says: "I have to hold this together." The other says: "I am held."

Jesus said: "Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat or drink; or about your body, what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothes? Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?" Matthew six, twenty-five through twenty-seven.
Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?

The answer is no. We know this. And yet we keep worrying — because worry feels like doing something. It feels productive. It tricks us into thinking that if we just think about it hard enough, anticipate every possible failure, run every scenario... we can prevent the bad thing from happening.

We can't. Control is an illusion. And it costs us everything — our sleep, our peace, our presence, our joy. We spend so much energy holding the world together that we have nothing left for actually living in it.
Proverbs three, verses five and six: "Trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways submit to him, and he will make your paths straight."

Lean not on your own understanding. That's the hard part, isn't it? Because our understanding is all we have. Our analysis, our planning, our predicting. And the verse says: don't lean on that. Lean on something else.

This is not about being passive. It's not about doing nothing and hoping for the best. It's about redirecting trust. From yourself — from your capacity to manage every variable — to God. Who sees what you can't. Who holds what you can't. Who is already in the future you're trying to control.
Let's go back to your hands. Feel them open on your knees or at your sides. Palms up.

I want you to take the thing you named earlier — the thing you're gripping — and imagine placing it in your open hands. Not throwing it away. Not pretending it doesn't matter. Placing it there. Gently. Like something valuable that you're choosing to stop clutching.
Feel the weight of it in your open palms. And then... offer it upward. Not with force. With willingness. A quiet gesture that says: "I cannot carry this alone anymore. I was never supposed to."

This is surrender. And surrender is terrifying. Because it means admitting that you are not in control. That you cannot guarantee the outcome. That the thing you love most in the world might not go the way you planned.
Let that fear be here. Don't push it away. Surrender that isn't afraid isn't surrender — it's indifference. Real surrender feels like stepping off a ledge. And trusting that the ground will meet you.

Feel your open hands. Feel the vulnerability of that position. Palms up. Undefended. This is not weakness. This is the most courageous posture a human being can take — to stop pretending you're in charge.
The Christian tradition has long practiced what some call "the prayer of relinquishment." It's simple. You hold your hands open before God and say: "Into your hands."
That's it. Not a long prayer. Not a sophisticated theology. Just... into your hands.
Try it now. Inwardly. "Into your hands."
One more time. Slowly. Feel each word. "Into... your... hands."

This doesn't mean the worry disappears. You may close your hands again in ten minutes, an hour, tomorrow morning. That's fine. That's human. The practice is not achieving permanent surrender. The practice is noticing when you're gripping — and opening again. And again. And again. As many times as it takes.
Each opening is an act of trust. Each opening is a prayer.
Let me pray over you now. Keep your hands open if you can.
Lord... we come to you with open hands. And that is harder than it sounds. Because we have been gripping things so tightly, for so long, that our fingers have forgotten how to straighten.
We bring you the things we've named. The outcomes we're trying to force. The people we're trying to fix. The futures we're trying to guarantee. The illusion of control that exhausts us daily.
We don't hand these to you because they don't matter. We hand them to you because they matter so much that our grip has become a prison.

Teach us to trust you. Not the easy trust of a problem that resolved itself. The hard trust of an open hand in an uncertain world. The trust that says: I don't know what happens next, but I know who holds it.
"Trust in the Lord with all your heart." We want to. Help us where we can't.
Into your hands we place what we cannot carry. Into your hands we release what we cannot control. Into your hands.
Stay here for a moment. Feel your body. Feel your open hands. Feel the air on your palms.

When you're ready, begin to close your hands slowly. Not into fists — into softly curled fingers. A relaxed hand. Neither gripping nor straining to stay open. This is the goal: not permanent surrender, but a hand that knows how to open. That remembers the feel of letting go.
Take a slow breath. Feel your feet on the floor. The chair beneath you. The weight of your body, supported.
You are not holding the world together. You never were. And the One who is... is not struggling.
